ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents cooked veal meat broadly; no specific baked veal leg code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for baked veal leg; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of baked veal leg is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion of cooked boneless meat.

nutrient_source.title: Veal, leg (top round), separable lean and fat, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06030003, confidence 0.95). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted veal leg (lean and fat) used as secondary cross-reference and to supply fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat) not reported in HPB. HPB values take precedence for core macros and micronutrients where available.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baked veal leg contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The numeric value 0 is used here as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to signal that GI measurement is meaningless for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (68 mg/100 g) and high potassium (389 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Baked veal leg is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt, high-sodium marinades, or salty gravies; its naturally low sodium and high potassium content support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content means no glycaemic impact; high protein (27.7 g/100 g) slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked veal leg is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food that does not raise blood glucose; pair with low-GI vegetables and avoid sweet glazes or sauces that add sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (103 mg/100 g) and presence of saturated fat (~1.62 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) require portion awareness; however, total fat is low at 4.65 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may include baked veal leg in moderate portions (up to 120 g) as part of a heart-healthy diet given its low total fat content, but should limit frequency due to cholesterol and trace trans fat, and avoid adding saturated-fat-rich sauces or butter during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat classification with arachidonic acid (omega-6) content that amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA; trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including veal to no more than 1–2 servings per week, prefer omega-3-rich fish as a protein alternative, and avoid high-heat charring which increases advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked veal leg is a lean, pale red meat dish prepared from the boneless hind leg portion of a young calf, oven-roasted until fully cooked through. The meat is tender with a mild, delicate flavour and a firm yet moist texture, retaining both internal and external fat layers that contribute to juiciness. Veal is classified as red meat and is widely consumed in European cuisines, particularly Italian, French, and German culinary traditions. Per 100 g, baked veal leg provides 160 kcal, 27.7 g protein, 4.65 g total fat (including approximately 1.62 g saturated fat and trace trans fat of 0.1 g), and 0.0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is very low at 68 mg/100 g and potassium is high at 389 mg/100 g, supporting DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is moderate at 103 mg/100 g. The food is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero glycaemic impact, suitable for hypertension when prepared without added salt, and should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its cholesterol, saturated fat, and pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06030003, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Fatty acid sub-fractions cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for roasted veal leg (lean and fat), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Both sources are on the approved list.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: veal leg is a pure muscle meat with no carbohydrate content by composition; confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Foodo database. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food containing no plant fibre. Glycemic index: this food contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; GI measurement is not applicable. The numeric value 0 is stored in glycemic_index to satisfy the requirement that the field must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to signal that GI is meaningless for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Saturated fat (1.62 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (1.74 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.37 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (roasted veal leg, lean and fat) as HPB does not report fatty acid sub-fractions. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, mineral sub-fraction, and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy provides partial micronutrient data for veal leg but values were not incorporated as the HPB record is the primary source and cross-mapping would require explicit adaptation documentation beyond current scope. Food subgroup corrected from 'Beef' (input error) to 'Veal' as this food is from young calf, not bovine cattle. Halal status noted in dietary_tags from input; kosher status not confirmed and set to false. Trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is a trace amount estimated from USDA data; this is noted as a pro-inflammatory factor relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Baked Veal Leg', a precise canonical English name. No multilingual requirement applies as this is a Western/European dish, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish. G6 is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (160.0), protein_g (27.7), fat_g (4.65), carbohydrate_g (0.0) all present and numeric. Carbohydrate zero is justified in notes as naturally zero for muscle meat, confirmed by HPB. nutrient_source clearly names HPB Foodo as primary and USDA SR Legacy as secondary cross-reference.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero for animal muscle food), sodium_mg (68.0), saturated_fat_g (1.62 — adapted from USDA SR Legacy), sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero) all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric value 0 (satisfying the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric as flagged by the validator); gi_category set to not_applicable to signal GI is meaningless for this zero-carbohydrate food; glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0; gi_source present with full explanation. The notes field documents the rationale for using 0 as a numeric sentinel for a not-applicable GI context.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for veal leg food concept. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (cooked veal meat) used as closest_parent match with gap note. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 7 English-language variants including alternate names, regional variants, and descriptive forms. aliases_by_language english array matches flat aliases exactly. No multilingual aliases required for Western/European cuisine. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language.english appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. Ensured glycemic_index was null for negligible carbohydrate foods.

Iteration 3: Validator flagged q4 failure — 'glycemic_index must be numeric'. Changed glycemic_index from null to numeric value 0 to satisfy the validator's numeric type requirement. gi_category remains not_applicable and notes document the sentinel-value rationale. This resolves the q4 failure from iteration 2.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Baked Veal Leg record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The iteration 2 draft failed q4 because the validator requires glycemic_index to be a numeric value; null was rejected. In iteration 3, glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 with gi_category not_applicable and full documentation in notes and gi_source to clarify that GI measurement is not meaningful for this zero-carbohydrate food. Primary nutrient data sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (approved primary source, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary source). Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and fibre values are justified as naturally zero for animal muscle meat. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible and honest. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. No multilingual requirement applies for Western/European cuisine.
